triadic_ontology.findings: Damiano Migliorini's paper 'Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk' offers a rigorous and original inquiry into the possibility of formulating a coherent trinitarian theism through the development of a relational ontology and a metaphysics grounded in the doctrine of the Trinity. Denis Bailey's paper 'A Relational Cosmos Requires the Christian God' argues that a universe structured by relation, coherence, and emergent order requires an originating source capable of generating distinction without division, unity without collapse, and mediation without hierarchy. Mark Pelley's paper 'Reality Is Triune: A Metaphysical Argument for Agápe as First Principle' argues that reality cannot be grounded in solitary being, neutral structure, or dyadic relation, and that the only mode of relation capable of sustaining coherent existence is agápe, which requires a triune structure. The Trinitarian Standard Model, presented by Coherent Intelligence, proposes a [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Recent advancements in multi-agent AI research systems have led to the development of autonomous research pipelines and self-evolving agents. The Mimosa framework, for instance, enables the evolution of multi-agent systems for scientific research, while OMNI-SIMPLEMEM is a unified multimodal memory framework for lifelong AI agents. Additionally, CORAL is a robust infrastructure for multi-agent autonomous self-evolution, built for autoresearch. Furthermore, prompt engineering has become a crucial aspect of reasoning models, with techniques such as adaptive thinking, reasoning effort levels, and Deep Think's tool-use chains being used to control effort, not technique. However, old tricks like chain-of-thought now hurt performance on these models, and it's essential to let them reason internally and prompt for outcomes, not steps.

uap.findings: Recent developments in Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP) disclosure, congressional hearings, and non-human intelligence declassification indicate a push for greater transparency alongside continued official caution regarding extraterrestrial conclusions. The U.S. government has initiated a significant effort to declassify and release UAP-related files, with the Department of War releasing previously secret files in May 2026. Congressional hearings have also shown increased interest in UAPs, with top military officials testifying and advocating for greater transparency. However, despite the increased transparency, there is no compelling evidence of alien intelligence, and many reported sightings can be attributed to weather phenomena or misidentified conventional objects.

classroom_ready.title: Token Boards in the High School Classroom
classroom_ready.summary: A token board is a visual reinforcement system that allows students to earn tokens for completing tasks or displaying appropriate behavior. Once students collect enough tokens, they exchange them for a preferred item, activity, or privilege.

classroom_ready.title: Engaging Students with an AI-Proof Choice-Based Curriculum
classroom_ready.summary: A choice-based curriculum gives students agency and increases engagement. Within each unit, they have 4-5 texts to choose from. Rather than examining one text together, we examine one theme across multiple texts.

classroom_ready.title: Teaching Romeo and Juliet Through a Research Lens
classroom_ready.summary: This unit is my attempt to reconcile three things: secondary students still need explicit instruction in reading, writing, and thinking; Shakespeare is part of the canon that students are expected to encounter in high school; and I’ve got too many standards to cover to spend weeks on Shakespeare without having it overlap with something else.

classroom_ready.title: Classroom Token System: 8 ABA Steps to Reinforce Appropriate Behavior
classroom_ready.summary: A classroom token system is one of the most flexible applied behavior analysis tools you can use with your students. When set up correctly, token systems help students understand the connection between their behavior and reinforcement, even when that reinforcement isn’t immediate.

pedagogy_pieces.title: The Importance of Individualization in Token Systems
pedagogy_pieces.summary: One of the core principles of applied behavior analysis is individualization. The number of tokens students need to earn and the reinforcer they are earning must be individualized. We need to make sure that every student wins.

pedagogy_pieces.title: Using Research to Inform Instruction
pedagogy_pieces.summary: This unit is my attempt to reconcile three things: secondary students still need explicit instruction in reading, writing, and thinking; Shakespeare is part of the canon that students are expected to encounter in high school; and I’ve got too many standards to cover to spend weeks on Shakespeare without having it overlap with something else.

english_lesson_plans.notes: These resources provide a range of strategies for engaging students and promoting positive behavior in the classroom. The token economy system can be an effective tool for reinforcing appropriate behavior, and individualization is key to ensuring that every student is successful. Additionally, using research to inform instruction can help teachers create more effective and engaging lesson plans.

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: The Education of Pope Leo XIV by Greg Grandin discusses the life and work of Pope Leo XIV, who was born as Father Bob Prevost and became the Pope in 2023. The article explores his early life, his experiences as a priest, and his rise to the papacy. It also delves into his views on liberation theology and his efforts to reform the Catholic Church.

ProPublica's article 'Mass Arrests by Trump's ICE, CBP Repeatedly Fail Under Scrutiny' investigates the arrests of protesters and bystanders during immigration sweeps and accuses them of crimes like assaulting or interfering with law enforcement. The article finds that over and over, cases against protesters fell apart, often because statements made by the arresting officers were debunked by video footage.

The Marginalian's article 'Hannah Arendt on the Power of Being an Outsider' explores Arendt's ideas on the importance of being an outsider and how it can lead to a deeper understanding of humanity. The article discusses Arendt's concept of 'dark times' and how they can be overcome through the power of fraternity and solidarity among marginalized groups.

Jewish Currents' article 'The Limits of Diasporism' discusses the concept of diasporism and its relation to left Jewish identity. The article explores the history of diasporism and its connection to internationalism, and argues that it might sap substance from anti-Zionism.

Artforum's article 'Harmon Siegel and Rosalind Krauss on the Legacy of Art Criticism' discusses the legacy of art criticism and the role of critics in shaping our understanding of art. The article explores the history of art criticism and the ways in which it has evolved over time, and features an interview with Harmon Siegel and Rosalind Krauss.

newyorker.title: The Useless Beauty of Christo and Jeanne-Claude
newyorker.dek: Plans for a forgotten art work by the late duo were found in an airshaft. Now, with the help of Christo’s nephew, “Air Package on a Ceiling” is finally on view, at Gagosian. Anna Russell writes.
newyorker.text:
In 1964, the married artists Christo and Jeanne-Claude moved out of the Chelsea Hotel and into the top two floors of a loft on Howard Street, in SoHo. Sure, they were broke—the hotel proprietor said they could pay him back later—but they were jazzed about their new digs. The place had big windows and an airshaft. (Later, they bought the building; it was the seventies.) Eventually, the airshaft became a storage unit, and it was there, in 2018, that Christo’s studio manager discovered a scale model, complete with electrical wiring and preparatory drawings, for a project the artist had forgotten about from half a century earlier. Eureka!
The other day, at the Gagosian in London, several art installers were hoisting ropes and clambering up ladders, in order to mount the work in a gallery for the first time. The piece, “Air Package on a Ceiling,” was designed in 1968 for the Institute of Contemporary Art, in Philadelphia, and involves a giant sheet of plastic suspended from the ceiling and crossed by ropes that make it bulge and fold. (It never reached the I.C.A., owing to technical concerns.) The effect is disorienting, as if the air had turned solid. Overseeing things was Christo’s nephew, Vladimir Yavachev, who was dressed in layers of black. “I mean, it’s up there—it’s not falling,” he said, peering at the plastic.
Yavachev began working with Christo in 1990, when he was a teen-ager, and newly freed from the constraints of Communist Bulgaria. He arrived in New York having never met his uncle, who had escaped the Eastern Bloc years earlier, by bribing a customs officer, and had never returned. “In Communist Bulgaria, there was a word for it,” Yavachev said. “You don’t become an immigrant, you become a nevuzvrashtenetz, which means ‘the one that can never come back.’ ” Christo and Jeanne-Claude travelled the world wrapping things: cars, islands, the Sydney coastline. “The enthusiasm was incredible,” Yavachev said.
Christo died in 2020—Jeanne-Claude in 2009—but the duo left detailed instructions for the posthumous installation of “Air Package” and other works, including “L’Arc de Triomphe, Wrapped,” which Yavachev completed in 2021. At eighty, Christo was involved in the final planning. By the time the installation began, his presence was greatly missed. “He had always been like a jackrabbit running around to see a project from every point of view that you can,” Yavachev said. “The way he would look at them, it was like a little kid looking at a cake, like you want to eat it. It was always like this, always ‘Look, look, look, look!’ ”
In the gallery, a man in a ball cap had climbed a ladder to examine the folds of the plastic sheet. “More slack,” someone said. “More folds.” Yavachev had flown in that morning from Abu Dhabi, where he has been working on realizing the duo’s most ambitious work, “The Mastaba,” a tomblike structure made of oil barrels in the desert, which, if completed, would be one of the world’s largest contemporary sculptures by volume. “Inshallah,” Yavachev said. He has been working on it for twenty years, with no end date in sight.
“Jeanne-Claude always used to say we do these projects for ourselves, and if other people enjoy it, that’s a bonus,” he went on. Many of the works were dreamed up decades before they became reality. “The projects find their time,” he said. He recalled a phrase he had learned from Bono, on the roof of the Arc de Triomphe. “Cathedral thinking,” he said. “Cathedral thinking is when you start something that you know that you cannot finish. Any architect of a cathedral, it will take two hundred years to finish a cathedral! You’re never going to see it. It’s very similar.”
In their lifetimes, the artists scrupulously avoided assigning meaning to their art works. “Christo always said, ‘I did not invent the politics in the Reichstag,’ ” Yavachev said. (They once wrapped the Reichstag.) “Coming from a totalitarian country, he never wanted to use art as propaganda. It was always art for art’s sake, just as a visual medium without any message. Though the message is there and anyone’s interpretation is legitimate.” “L’Arc de Triomphe, Wrapped” became a symbol of post-Covid unity, even though the piece was envisioned back in 1961.
“The power of this, and why it’s so strong, is because it’s an art work and because it’s useless,” Yavachev said. “Nobody needs it. This uselessness of stuff is the thing that makes us human.” What did he think visitors to the desert would make of “The Mastaba” in five, ten, or twenty years, once it was complete? “Oh, no, no,” he said. “I don’t like to speculate.” ♦

literary_pick.title: Orbital
literary_pick.author: Samantha Harvey
literary_pick.summary: Orbital is a novel about a seductive and cunning American woman who infiltrates an anarchist collective in France. The story follows her journey as she becomes entranced by a mysterious figure named Bruno Lacombe, a mentor to the young activists who believes that the path to emancipation is not revolt but a return to the ancient past.
